schools.summary: Gill: the LORD spake unto Moses, in order to deliver the same to Aaron, who, and the priests his successors, were chiefly concerned in the cleansing of the leper; the witnesses note God speaking to Moses to give the law for cleansing the leper — the opening of the rite of cleansing, to be carried out by the priest (God appointing the way the leper is cleansed: 'the LORD spake unto Moses, saying'; that the law was given to Moses in order to deliver the same to Aaron, the priests being chiefly concerned in this cleansing; that the cleansing of the leper, like all the holy rites, was of God's own appointment, none devising the way but God; the meaning the commentators draw, that the cleansing of the sinner, of whom the leper is a type, is wholly of God's ordering, the way of cleansing being his, not man's; that, as the leper could not cleanse himself, so God appointed the means; that the way of cleansing from sin is revealed by God).

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses
saying
the LORD speaking to Moses to give the law for cleansing the leper, carried out by the priests
the cleansing of God's appointment, a type of the cleansing of the sinner, the leper unable to cleanse himself

schools.summary: Gill: this shall be the law of the leper, the rules, rites, ceremonies, and sacrifices to be observed in the day of his cleansing; he was to be brought unto the priest; the witnesses note God giving the law of the leper for the day of his cleansing, that he be brought to the priest — the appointing of the rite of cleansing, the leper presented to the priest (the leper brought to the priest to be cleansed: 'this shall be the law of the leper in the day of his cleansing: He shall be brought unto the priest'; the law, 'the law of the leper in the day of his cleansing,' the rites and sacrifices for the cleansing; the bringing, 'he shall be brought unto the priest,' that the leper, unable to come of himself, was brought to the priest, who alone could pronounce on his case; the great type the commentators draw, that the leper is a figure of the sinner, defiled, spreading his uncleanness, and put out from the camp, and the cleansing, a figure of the cleansing of the sinner; that the sinner must be brought to the great High Priest, Christ, who alone cleanses and pronounces clean; that there is a day of cleansing, a set time of grace, when the defiled is made clean; that the way to be cleansed is to be brought to the priest).

debates.question: What does the leper and his cleansing typify?

sides.position: The leper is a figure of the sinner — defiled, spreading his uncleanness, and put out from the camp; the cleansing a figure of the cleansing of the sinner, who must be brought to the great High Priest, Christ, who alone cleanses and pronounces clean; there is a day of cleansing, a set time of grace

themes: This shall be the law of the leper in the day of his cleansing
He shall be brought unto the priest
the law of the leper for the day of his cleansing, that he be brought to the priest
the leper a figure of the sinner, brought to the great High Priest, Christ, who alone cleanses and pronounces clean

schools.summary: Gill: the priest shall go forth out of the camp, a little without it, to the leper, who was shut out, and shall look whether the plague of leprosy be healed; the witnesses note the priest going out of the camp to the leper and looking whether the leprosy is healed — the priest's going forth to the outcast, and the looking for healing (the priest going out to the leper shut out: 'the priest shall go forth out of the camp; and the priest shall look, and, behold, if the plague of leprosy be healed in the leper'; the going forth, 'the priest shall go forth out of the camp,' that the priest went out a little without it, to the leper who was shut out of the camp; the looking, 'the priest shall look... if the plague... be healed,' that the priest examined whether the healing was already wrought; that the healing went before the cleansing, the leper being first healed by God, then declared clean by the rite; the great type the commentators draw, that the priest going forth out of the camp to the outcast leper shadows Christ, who comes forth to the outcast sinner, even outside the camp, to seek and cleanse him; that God first heals, then the cleansing is pronounced; that Christ goes out to the cast-out to save).

themes: the priest shall go forth out of the camp; and the priest shall look
if the plague of leprosy be healed in the leper
the priest going out of the camp to the leper, shut out, to look whether the leprosy is healed
a type of Christ, who comes forth to the outcast sinner, even outside the camp (Hebrews 13:12-13); God first heals, then the cleansing is pronounced

schools.summary: Gill: the priest commanded to take for him that is to be cleansed two birds alive and clean, with cedar wood, scarlet, and hyssop; the witnesses note the priest commanding to take for the leper two living clean birds, cedar wood, scarlet, and hyssop — the gathering of the materials of the cleansing rite (these things taken for the leper's cleansing: 'the priest shall command to take for him that is to be cleansed two birds alive and clean, and cedar wood, and scarlet, and hyssop'; the birds, 'two birds alive and clean,' two living clean birds, for the rite about to be described; the cedar, scarlet, and hyssop, 'cedar wood, and scarlet, and hyssop,' the cedar, lasting wood, the scarlet, the worm-dyed thread, and the hyssop, the lowly herb, used in the sprinkling; the meaning the commentators draw, that the two birds shadow Christ in his death and resurrection, and that the cedar, scarlet, and hyssop, as in other purifyings, are the instruments of cleansing; that the hyssop especially is the herb of purging, purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean; that the materials of cleansing are appointed of God, every one with its meaning).

themes: the priest shall command to take for him that is to be cleansed two birds alive and clean
and cedar wood, and scarlet, and hyssop
two living clean birds, cedar wood, scarlet, and hyssop taken for the leper's cleansing
the two birds shadowing Christ's death and resurrection; the hyssop the herb of purging ('purge me with hyssop,' Psalm 51:7)

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: one of the birds was to be killed in an earthen vessel over fresh (running) water, so that its blood ran into the water; the witnesses note the priest commanding one of the birds to be killed in an earthen vessel over running water — the slaying of the one bird, its blood mingled with living water (the first bird killed over running water: 'the priest shall command that one of the birds be killed in an earthen vessel over running water'; the killing, 'one of the birds be killed,' that one of the two living birds was slain; the manner, 'in an earthen vessel over running water,' that it was killed over a vessel of fresh, living, running water, so that the blood ran into and mingled with the water; the great type the commentators draw, that the slain bird shadows Christ in his death, the earthen vessel his human nature, we have this treasure in earthen vessels, and the running water the Spirit, or the living water that flows from his death; that the blood mingled with living water shadows the blood and water that flowed from Christ's pierced side; that the death of the one bird is the ground of the cleansing; that the cleansing of the sinner rests on the death of Christ).

debates.question: What does the bird killed in an earthen vessel over running water shadow?

sides.position: Christ in his death — the slain bird his death, the earthen vessel his human nature ('this treasure in earthen vessels,' 2 Corinthians 4:7), the running water the Spirit and the living water that flows from his death; the blood mingled with water shadowing the blood and water from his pierced side (John 19:34), the death of the bird the ground of the cleansing

themes: the priest shall command that one of the birds be killed in an earthen vessel
over running water
one of the birds killed in an earthen vessel over running water, its blood mingled with the living water
the slain bird shadowing Christ's death, the earthen vessel his human nature, the blood and water from his pierced side (John 19:34)

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the living bird, with the cedar wood, the scarlet, and the hyssop, was to be dipped in the blood of the bird that was killed over the running water; the witnesses note the priest taking the living bird and the cedar, scarlet, and hyssop, and dipping them in the blood of the slain bird — the dipping of the living bird in the blood of the slain (the living bird dipped in the blood of the dead: 'he shall take it, and the cedar wood, and the scarlet, and the hyssop, and shall dip them and the living bird in the blood of the bird that was killed over the running water'; the living bird, 'the living bird,' the second of the two, taken alive; the dipping, 'shall dip them and the living bird in the blood,' that the living bird, with the cedar, scarlet, and hyssop, was dipped in the blood of the bird slain over the running water; the great type the commentators draw, that the living bird, dipped in the blood of the slain and then let loose, shadows Christ in his resurrection, who, having died, rose again bearing the virtue of his death, and the believer raised and set free by the applied blood; that the two birds together, the one slain and the one living, set forth the one Christ in his death and his rising; that the living bird must be dipped in the blood, the resurrection-life resting on the death).

debates.question: What does the living bird, dipped in the blood and then let loose, shadow?

sides.position: Christ in his resurrection — who, having died, rose again bearing the virtue of his death; the two birds together, the one slain and the one living, setting forth the one Christ in his death and his rising, the believer raised and set free by the applied blood, the resurrection-life resting on the death

themes: he shall take it, and the cedar wood, and the scarlet, and the hyssop, and shall dip them and the living bird in the blood
of the bird that was killed over the running water
the living bird, with cedar, scarlet, and hyssop, dipped in the blood of the slain bird
the living bird shadowing Christ's resurrection bearing the virtue of his death; the two birds the one Christ slain and risen

schools.summary: Gill: he shall sprinkle upon him that is to be cleansed seven times, with the hyssop fastened to the cedar stick, with the scarlet, dipped in the blood, and shall pronounce him clean, and let the living bird loose into the open field; the witnesses note the priest sprinkling the leper seven times with the blood, pronouncing him clean, and letting the living bird loose into the open field — the sprinkling, the pronouncing clean, and the loosing of the living bird (the blood sprinkled and the bird set free: 'he shall sprinkle upon him that is to be cleansed from the leprosy seven times, and shall pronounce him clean, and shall let the living bird loose into the open field'; the sprinkling, 'sprinkle... seven times,' that the blood, with the hyssop, cedar, and scarlet, was sprinkled on the leper seven times, the perfect number; the pronouncing, 'shall pronounce him clean,' that, the blood applied, the priest declared him clean; the loosing, 'let the living bird loose into the open field,' that the living bird, bearing the blood, was let go free; the great type the commentators draw, that the blood of Christ, applied, sprinkled, by the Spirit, cleanses the sinner, who is then pronounced clean, and the living bird, set free into the open field, shadows Christ risen and ascended, or the cleansed soul set at liberty; that the applied blood cleanses, and the cleansed is set free; that there is full and perfect cleansing by the blood).

themes: he shall sprinkle upon him that is to be cleansed from the leprosy seven times, and shall pronounce him clean
and shall let the living bird loose into the open field
the leper sprinkled seven times with the blood, pronounced clean, and the living bird let loose
the blood of Christ applied by the Spirit cleansing the sinner; the living bird set free shadowing Christ risen and ascended, the soul at liberty

schools.summary: Gill: he that is to be cleansed shall wash his clothes, that there be no remains of the infection, and shave off all his hair, and wash himself, that he may be clean, and come into the camp, but tarry out of his tent seven days; the witnesses note the cleansed leper washing his clothes, shaving all his hair, and washing himself, that he be clean, then coming into the camp but staying out of his tent seven days — the personal cleansing of the leper, and his partial readmission (sprinkled, he now washes and shaves to be clean: 'he that is to be cleansed shall wash his clothes, and shave off all his hair, and wash himself in water, that he may be clean: and after that he shall come into the camp, and shall tarry abroad out of his tent seven days'; the washing, 'wash his clothes... and wash himself,' that no remains of the infection might cling; the shaving, 'shave off all his hair,' a putting away of the old; the partial readmission, 'come into the camp, and shall tarry abroad out of his tent seven days,' admitted to the camp, yet not yet to his own tent, for seven days; the meaning the commentators draw, that the cleansed sinner is to put away all the old defilement, washing and shaving, and that the cleansing is by degrees, not all at once; that the believer, cleansed by the blood, is also to cleanse himself from all filthiness; that there is a full putting away of the old in true cleansing).

themes: he that is to be cleansed shall wash his clothes, and shave off all his hair, and wash himself in water, that he may be clean
and shall tarry abroad out of his tent seven days
the cleansed leper washing, shaving all his hair, and washing himself, then admitted to the camp but tarrying out of his tent seven days
a full putting away of the old defilement, the cleansing by degrees; the cleansed sinner, washed by the blood, also to cleanse himself

schools.summary: Gill: on the seventh day, after he was first brought to the priest and cleansed by the two birds, he shall shave all his hair, his head, beard, and eyebrows, and wash his clothes and his flesh, and be clean; the witnesses note God appointing that on the seventh day the leper shave off all his hair, head, beard, and eyebrows, and wash his clothes and flesh, and so be clean — the second and complete shaving and washing, the finishing of the cleansing (on the seventh day he is wholly shaven and washed: 'on the seventh day... he shall shave all his hair off his head and his beard and his eyebrows... and he shall wash his clothes, also he shall wash his flesh in water, and he shall be clean'; the time, 'the seventh day,' the day completing the week of his tarrying; the shaving, 'shave all his hair... his head and his beard and his eyebrows, even all his hair,' a full and thorough shaving, leaving nothing of the old; the washing, 'wash his clothes... wash his flesh,' the full washing; the result, 'he shall be clean,' that now he is wholly clean; the meaning the commentators draw, that the cleansing is thorough and complete, nothing of the old defilement left, the whole man washed and renewed; that true cleansing leaves no part untouched, all the old put away; that the believer is to be wholly cleansed, body and spirit).

themes: on the seventh day... he shall shave all his hair off his head and his beard and his eyebrows... and he shall wash his clothes, also he shall wash his flesh in water
and he shall be clean
on the seventh day the leper shaves off all his hair and washes his clothes and flesh, and is clean
a full and thorough cleansing, nothing of the old defilement left, the believer to be wholly cleansed, body and spirit

schools.summary: Gill: on the eighth day, from the leper's first appearance and the day after the former things were done, he shall take two he lambs without blemish, one ewe lamb, fine flour for a meat offering, and a log of oil; the witnesses note God appointing that on the eighth day the cleansed leper bring two he lambs, an ewe lamb, fine flour mingled with oil, and a log of oil — the offerings of the eighth day, the sacrifices that complete the cleansing (he brings the lambs, the flour, and the oil: 'on the eighth day he shall take two he lambs without blemish, and one ewe lamb... and three tenth deals of fine flour for a meat offering, mingled with oil, and one log of oil'; the time, 'the eighth day,' after the seven days of his cleansing; the lambs, 'two he lambs... and one ewe lamb... without blemish,' for the trespass, sin, and burnt offerings; the flour and oil, 'fine flour... mingled with oil, and one log of oil,' the meat offering and the oil for the rite; the meaning the commentators draw, that the spotless lambs shadow Christ, the Lamb without blemish, and the oil the Spirit; that the cleansed leper, restored, brings his offerings to God, all the offerings of atonement and thanksgiving; that the cleansing is completed by sacrifice, pointing to the one offering of Christ).

themes: on the eighth day he shall take two he lambs without blemish, and one ewe lamb... and three tenth deals of fine flour... mingled with oil
and one log of oil
the cleansed leper bringing on the eighth day two he lambs, an ewe lamb, fine flour, and oil for the offerings
the spotless lambs shadowing Christ, the Lamb without blemish, and the oil the Spirit; the cleansing completed by sacrifice

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the priest who makes him clean shall present the man and the offerings before the LORD, at the entrance of the tabernacle; the witnesses note the priest presenting the cleansed man and his offerings before the LORD at the door of the tabernacle — the presenting of the man and his gifts before God (the man and his offerings set before the LORD: 'the priest that maketh him clean shall present the man that is to be made clean, and those things, before the LORD, at the door of the tabernacle'; the priest, 'the priest that maketh him clean,' that the priest, by the rites and sacrifices, was the means of his cleansing; the presenting, 'shall present the man... and those things, before the LORD,' that both the man and his offerings were brought before God; the place, 'at the door of the tabernacle,' the appointed place of God's presence; the meaning the commentators draw, that the cleansed sinner is presented before God by the priest, even by Christ, who presents his own to God; that we are brought near to God only by the priest and the sacrifice; that the cleansed is set before the LORD, to be made wholly clean).

themes: the priest that maketh him clean shall present the man that is to be made clean, and those things, before the LORD
at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation
the priest presenting the cleansed man and his offerings before the LORD at the door
a type of the cleansed sinner presented before God by the priest, even by Christ; we brought near only by the priest and the sacrifice

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the wave-offering: the priest takes one he lamb and offers it for a trespass offering, with the log of oil, and waves them before the LORD; the witnesses note the priest taking one lamb for a trespass offering, with the log of oil, and waving them before the LORD — the trespass offering of the cleansed leper, waved before God (the lamb and the oil waved as a trespass offering: 'the priest shall take one he lamb, and offer him for a trespass offering, and the log of oil, and wave them for a wave offering before the LORD'; the offering, 'one he lamb... for a trespass offering,' that the first lamb was for a trespass, guilt, offering; the waving, 'the log of oil, and wave them for a wave offering,' that the lamb and the oil were waved to and fro before the LORD, presented to God; the meaning the commentators draw, that the trespass offering shadows Christ bearing the guilt of the sinner, and that the leper's restoration begins with the offering for guilt; that the cleansed sinner needs atonement for his trespass, and that the waving presents it to God; that all restoration to God rests on the offering for guilt).

themes: the priest shall take one he lamb, and offer him for a trespass offering, and the log of oil
and wave them for a wave offering before the LORD
the priest offering one lamb for a trespass offering, with the oil, and waving them before the LORD
the trespass offering shadowing Christ bearing the sinner's guilt; the restoration beginning with the offering for guilt

schools.summary: Gill: he shall slay the lamb in the place where he shall kill the sin offering and the burnt offering, in the holy place; for as the sin offering is the priest's, so is the trespass offering: it is most holy; the witnesses note the trespass lamb slain in the holy place, where the sin and burnt offerings were killed, the offering being the priest's and most holy — the slaying of the trespass offering in the holy place (it killed where the most holy offerings are: 'he shall slay the lamb in the place where he shall kill the sin offering and the burnt offering, in the holy place... it is most holy'; the place, 'the place where he shall kill the sin offering and the burnt offering, in the holy place,' the appointed place for the most holy sacrifices; the priest's portion, 'as the sin offering is the priest's, so is the trespass offering,' that it belonged to the priest; the holiness, 'it is most holy,' that the trespass offering was of the most holy sort; the meaning the commentators draw, that the most holy trespass offering shadows Christ, the most holy, offered for our guilt; that the place and the holiness mark the dignity and the sufficiency of the offering; that Christ, most holy, is the offering for our trespass).

themes: he shall slay the lamb in the place where he shall kill the sin offering and the burnt offering, in the holy place
it is most holy
the trespass lamb slain in the holy place, where the most holy offerings were killed
the most holy trespass offering shadowing Christ, the most holy, offered for our guilt

schools.summary: Gill: the priest shall take some of the blood of the trespass offering and put it upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and the great toe of his right foot; the witnesses note the priest putting the blood of the trespass offering on the right ear, thumb, and great toe of the cleansed leper — the blood on ear, hand, and foot, the cleansed leper consecrated as the priest was (the blood claiming all his powers: 'the priest shall take some of the blood of the trespass offering, and... put it upon the tip of the right ear... and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot'; the three places, 'the right ear... the thumb of the right hand... the great toe of the right foot,' the same as in the priest's consecration; the meaning before given, that the blood on the ear, hand, and foot signified that all his faculties were to be dedicated to God, what he hears, does, and walks; the great type the commentators draw, that the cleansed sinner, like the consecrated priest, is wholly claimed by the blood of Christ, his whole man sanctified, ear, hand, and foot; that the leper, once cast out, is now consecrated as if a priest, so full is his restoration; that the blood of Christ sanctifies the whole man).

debates.question: Why is the cleansed leper's ear, thumb, and toe touched with blood, as the priest's were?

sides.position: To signify that all his faculties are dedicated to God — what he hears, does, and walks; the cleansed sinner, like the consecrated priest, wholly claimed by the blood of Christ, his whole man sanctified; the leper, once cast out, now consecrated as if a priest, so full is his restoration

themes: the priest shall put it upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand
and upon the great toe of his right foot
the blood of the trespass offering put on the right ear, thumb, and great toe of the cleansed leper, as in the priest's consecration
the blood claiming all his faculties for God; the cleansed sinner consecrated as if a priest, his whole man sanctified by the blood of Christ

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the priest shall take some of the log of oil and pour it into the palm of his own left hand, to use in the rite that follows; the witnesses note the priest taking some of the oil and pouring it into the palm of his own left hand — the taking of the oil for the anointing of the cleansed (the oil made ready in the priest's hand: 'the priest shall take some of the log of oil, and pour it into the palm of his own left hand'; the oil, 'some of the log of oil,' taken from the log brought by the leper; the manner, 'pour it into the palm of his own left hand,' that the priest held the oil ready in his own hand for the rite; the meaning the commentators draw, that the oil, the type of the Spirit, was now to be applied, following the blood; that, the blood of atonement having been put on, the oil of the Spirit follows, for the Spirit is given after and upon the atonement; that the order is fixed: first the blood, then the oil, the atonement before the anointing; that there is no gift of the Spirit but upon the ground of the blood).

debates.question: What is signified by the oil (the Spirit) being applied after and upon the blood?

sides.position: That the Spirit is given after and upon the atonement — the order fixed: first the blood, then the oil, the atonement before the anointing; there is no gift of the Spirit but upon the ground of the blood

themes: the priest shall take some of the log of oil
and pour it into the palm of his own left hand
the priest pouring some of the oil into his own left palm, ready for the anointing
the oil (the Spirit) applied following the blood; the order fixed, first the blood then the oil, no gift of the Spirit but upon the ground of the blood

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the priest shall dip his right finger in the oil that is in his left hand, and sprinkle of the oil with his finger seven times before the LORD; the witnesses note the priest dipping his right finger in the oil and sprinkling it seven times before the LORD — the sevenfold sprinkling of the oil before God (the oil sprinkled seven times to the LORD: 'the priest shall dip his right finger in the oil that is in his left hand, and shall sprinkle of the oil with his finger seven times before the LORD'; the dipping, 'dip his right finger in the oil,' that the priest took the oil on his finger; the sprinkling, 'sprinkle of the oil... seven times before the LORD,' that the oil was sprinkled seven times, the perfect number, toward the LORD; the meaning the commentators draw, that the oil, the type of the Spirit, was first sprinkled before the LORD, the Spirit given of God and presented to him before being applied to the man; that the sevenfold sprinkling marks the fulness and perfection of the Spirit's grace; that the gifts of the Spirit are first before God, then given to men; that the full and perfect Spirit is presented to God for the cleansed).

themes: the priest shall dip his right finger in the oil that is in his left hand
and shall sprinkle of the oil with his finger seven times before the LORD
the priest dipping his finger in the oil and sprinkling it seven times before the LORD
the oil (the Spirit) first sprinkled before God; the sevenfold marking the fulness and perfection of the Spirit's grace

schools.summary: Gill: of the rest of the oil that is in his hand the priest shall put upon the tip of the right ear, the thumb of the right hand, and the great toe of the right foot, upon the blood of the trespass offering; the witnesses note the priest putting the oil on the right ear, thumb, and great toe of the cleansed, upon the blood of the trespass offering — the oil upon the blood, the Spirit following the atonement (the oil put exactly where the blood was: 'of the rest of the oil... shall the priest put upon the tip of the right ear... the thumb of his right hand, and... the great toe of his right foot, upon the blood of the trespass offering'; the three places, 'the right ear... the thumb... the great toe,' the same places as the blood; the key phrase, 'upon the blood of the trespass offering,' that the oil was put exactly upon the blood, the anointing upon the atonement; the great meaning the commentators draw, that the oil, the Spirit, was put upon the blood, the atonement, the Spirit and his graces following and resting upon the blood of Christ; that there is no anointing of the Spirit but upon the ground of the blood, the Spirit sanctifying where the blood has atoned; that the same faculties, atoned by the blood, are sanctified by the Spirit).

debates.question: What is signified by the oil being put exactly upon the blood on the ear, thumb, and toe?

sides.position: That the Spirit (the oil) follows and rests upon the atonement (the blood) — the Spirit and his graces resting upon the blood of Christ; there is no anointing of the Spirit but upon the ground of the blood, the same faculties, atoned by the blood, sanctified by the Spirit

themes: of the rest of the oil... shall the priest put upon the tip of the right ear... the thumb of his right hand, and... the great toe of his right foot
upon the blood of the trespass offering
the oil put on the ear, thumb, and toe of the cleansed, exactly upon the blood of the trespass offering
the oil (the Spirit) upon the blood (the atonement); no anointing of the Spirit but upon the ground of the blood of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the remnant of the oil that is in the priest's hand he shall put upon the head of him that is to be cleansed, and make an atonement for him before the LORD; the witnesses note the priest putting the rest of the oil on the head of the cleansed and making atonement for him before the LORD — the anointing of the head with the remaining oil, and the atonement (the oil crowning the cleansed and atonement made: 'the remnant of the oil that is in the priest's hand he shall put upon the head of him that is to be cleansed: and the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD'; the anointing, 'the remnant of the oil... upon the head,' that all the rest of the oil was poured on his head, the whole man, from ear to head, anointed; the atonement, 'make an atonement for him before the LORD,' that the rite made atonement for the cleansed; the meaning the commentators draw, that the cleansed sinner is wholly anointed with the Spirit, the oil flowing over his head, as the priest was anointed; that the full Spirit is given to the cleansed, crowning the whole work; that the atonement and the anointing together complete his cleansing; that the believer is anointed and atoned, sealed by the Spirit).

themes: the remnant of the oil that is in the priest's hand he shall put upon the head of him that is to be cleansed
and the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD
the rest of the oil put on the head of the cleansed and atonement made, the whole man anointed
the cleansed sinner wholly anointed with the Spirit (Psalm 133:2); the atonement and anointing completing his cleansing, the believer atoned and sealed

schools.summary: Gill: the priest shall offer the sin offering, this was the ewe lamb, according to the rite of every sin offering, and make atonement for him that is to be cleansed; and afterward kill the burnt offering; the witnesses note the priest offering the sin offering, the ewe lamb, to make atonement for the cleansed, then killing the burnt offering — the sin offering for atonement and the burnt offering following (the sin offering atoning and the burnt offering then offered: 'the priest shall offer the sin offering, and make an atonement for him that is to be cleansed from his uncleanness; and afterward he shall kill the burnt offering'; the sin offering, 'the sin offering... make an atonement... from his uncleanness,' the ewe lamb, atoning for his uncleanness; the order, 'afterward he shall kill the burnt offering,' that the burnt offering followed the sin offering; the meaning before given, that the sin offering shadows Christ atoning for our sin, and the burnt offering Christ's entire offering of himself, and the believer's whole devotion; that the cleansed, his sin atoned, is then wholly devoted to God; that atonement goes first, then consecration; that the order is ever: sin atoned, then the life given up to God).

themes: the priest shall offer the sin offering, and make an atonement for him that is to be cleansed from his uncleanness
and afterward he shall kill the burnt offering
the priest offering the sin offering to atone for the cleansed, then killing the burnt offering
the sin offering shadowing Christ atoning for sin, the burnt offering whole devotion; atonement first, then consecration

schools.summary: Gill: the priest shall offer the burnt offering and the meat offering, which belonged to it, upon the altar, and make atonement for him, and he shall be clean; the witnesses note the priest offering the burnt offering and the meat offering on the altar, making atonement, so that the leper is clean — the offering up of the burnt and meat offerings, and the cleansing completed (atonement made, the leper at last clean: 'the priest shall offer the burnt offering and the meat offering upon the altar: and the priest shall make an atonement for him, and he shall be clean'; the offerings, 'the burnt offering and the meat offering upon the altar,' the burnt offering of devotion, and the meat offering that belonged to it, offered to God; the atonement, 'make an atonement for him,' that the whole rite made full atonement; the end, 'he shall be clean,' that now, at the last, the leper is wholly clean; the meaning the commentators draw, that the whole work, blood and oil, sin, burnt, and meat offerings, made the cleansing complete, and that the cleansed is fully restored; that the sinner, atoned by the blood of Christ and anointed by the Spirit, is made wholly clean; that there is full cleansing and restoration in Christ).

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: here the provision made for the poor leper, that he might not be kept from the privilege of cleansing by his poverty; the witnesses note God making provision for the poor leper, that if he could not get so much, he should bring one lamb for a trespass offering, with a tenth deal of flour and a log of oil — the lessening of the offering for the poor, that none be shut out by poverty (the poor may bring less and yet be cleansed: 'if he be poor, and cannot get so much; then he shall take one lamb for a trespass offering... and one tenth deal of fine flour... and a log of oil'; the case, 'if he be poor, and cannot get so much,' as three lambs and three tenth deals; the lessened offering, 'one lamb for a trespass offering... one tenth deal of fine flour... and a log of oil,' the one lamb still required, but the meat offering and the rest reduced; the meaning the commentators draw, that God makes provision for the poor leper, that he might not be kept from the privilege of cleansing by his poverty; that the log of oil was not lessened, the Spirit given in full measure to the poor as to the rich; that the cleansing is open to the poorest, none shut out for poverty; that the grace of God in Christ is free to all, rich and poor alike).

debates.question: What does the provision for the poor leper teach?

sides.position: That none should be kept from the privilege of cleansing by poverty — the offering lessened, yet the log of oil not lessened, the Spirit given in full measure to poor and rich alike; the cleansing open to the poorest, the grace of God in Christ free to all

schools.summary: Gill: two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, such as he is able to get, as good as he can get for his money; the one for a sin offering, the other for a burnt offering; the witnesses note God allowing the poor leper to bring two doves or pigeons, one for a sin offering and one for a burnt offering, in place of the two lambs — the substitution of the birds for the lambs, for the poor (two birds serving in place of the lambs: 'two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, such as he is able to get; and the one shall be a sin offering, and the other a burnt offering'; the birds, 'two turtledoves, or two young pigeons,' such as he is able to get, as good as his money could buy; the use, 'the one... a sin offering, and the other a burnt offering,' the two birds standing for the ewe lamb and the second he lamb; the meaning before given, that God scaled the offering to the poor man's means, accepting the dove from the poor as the lamb from the rich; that the poorest, bringing his two doves, was as fully cleansed as the rich with his lambs; that Christ, the true offering, is as freely had by the poor as the rich; that God despises not the offering of the poor).

schools.summary: Gill: he shall bring them on the eighth day for his cleansing, which supposes him to have gone through all the former rites, unto the priest, at the door of the tabernacle; the witnesses note the poor leper bringing his offerings on the eighth day to the priest at the door of the tabernacle, before the LORD — the bringing of the poor man's offerings, on the same day and to the same place (the poor man too bringing his offering before the LORD: 'he shall bring them on the eighth day for his cleansing unto the priest, unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, before the LORD'; the time, 'on the eighth day for his cleansing,' the same eighth day as the rich, after the former rites; the place, 'unto the priest... unto the door of the tabernacle... before the LORD,' the same place and presence as the rich; the meaning the commentators draw, that the poor man's cleansing follows the same order, on the same day, at the same door, before the same LORD; that the poor are not put off to a lesser place or a lesser God, but brought to the same presence; that all, rich and poor, come to God the same way, by the priest and the offering; that there is one way of cleansing for all).

schools.summary: John Wesley: the priest shall put the blood upon the extremities of the body, the ear, the hand, and the foot, to signify the consecration of the whole man; the witnesses note the priest killing the poor man's trespass lamb and putting its blood on his right ear, thumb, and great toe — the same blood on ear, hand, and foot for the poor as for the rich (the poor man too consecrated by the blood: 'he shall kill the lamb of the trespass offering... and put it upon the tip of the right ear... and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot'; the slaying, 'kill the lamb of the trespass offering,' the one lamb slain; the blood, 'put it upon the tip of the right ear... thumb... great toe,' on the extremities of the body, signifying the consecration of the whole man; the meaning before given, that the blood on ear, hand, and foot dedicates the whole man to God, what he hears, does, and walks; that the poor man is as fully consecrated as the rich, his whole man claimed by the blood; that the blood of Christ sanctifies the poorest wholly, no less than the rich; that all the cleansed are wholly the Lord's).

schools.summary: God putting the plague of leprosy in a house: 'when ye be come into the land of Canaan, which I give to you for a possession, and I put the plague of leprosy in a house of the land of your possession' — the divine hand — 'I put the plague of leprosy in a house': when Israel came into Canaan, God himself would 'put' the plague of leprosy in a house (the witnesses noting the significance — God's own hand in the affliction, the plague not by chance but by God's appointment, 'I put' — God himself sending the plague, the affliction from his hand, the lesson — the divine providence in afflictions, God's hand in the troubles that come on our houses and possessions, the plague sent by him, whether in chastisement, warning, or trial, 'shall there be evil in a city, and the LORD hath not done it?' Amos 3:6, 'I form the light, and create darkness... I the LORD do all these things,' Isaiah 45:7, the comfort and the warning, the affliction from God's hand, to be received as from him, the house leprosy a visitation, some noting it might be a chastisement for the sin of the dwellers, or a warning, the figure — God's sovereign hand in the afflictions of his people, even in the plague in the house, all under his ordering, and the land given as a possession, yet even there the plague might come, the reminder that no possession is exempt from God's visitation); the land given as a possession (the inheritance, the gift of God), yet even there the plague might come (the visitation in the very land of promise) — the providence (the affliction even in the inheritance) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'I put the plague of leprosy in a house' teach?

sides.position: God's own hand in the affliction (the plague sent by his appointment, not by chance) - the divine providence in the troubles that come on our houses and possessions (Amos 3:6; Isaiah 45:7), the affliction to be received as from his hand, even in the land of promise no possession exempt from his visitation

debates.question: What does the emptying of the house teach?

sides.position: The merciful provision that the man's goods be spared, emptied before the verdict that would make all unclean - a figure of God's mercy and consideration even in his judgments (the gentleness that tempers severity, Habakkuk 3:2; Psalm 145:9), the mercy that does not make the loss greater than it must be
